title: Communicating AI Value
description: Learn to communicate the value, capabilities, and limitations of AI features to diverse stakeholders — from engineering teams to executives to customers.
learningObjectives: Tailor AI communication to different audiences — engineers, executives, and customers — using frameworks appropriate for each group's priorities
Frame AI capabilities honestly and compellingly without overpromising or creating unrealistic expectations
Handle skepticism, pushback, and misconceptions about AI with confidence and evidence-based responses

sections.title: Understanding Your Audience
sections.content:
Every stakeholder group thinks about AI differently. Engineers want to know how it works. Executives want to know what it's worth. Customers want to know what it does for them. Communicating AI value effectively means translating the same underlying capability into three fundamentally different stories.

**Talking to Engineers**

Engineers are your most technically literate audience, but that doesn't mean they want a deep dive on transformer architecture. What engineers care about when you present an AI feature:

• **Integration complexity** — How does this fit into the existing stack? What APIs are involved? What's the latency budget? Engineers want to understand the scope of work, not a sales pitch.
• **Failure modes** — What happens when Claude returns a bad response? What's the fallback? Engineers respect PMs who've thought about edge cases, not just the happy path.
• **Iteration model** — How will prompts be updated? Who owns prompt quality? Is there a testing framework? Engineers want to know the maintenance story, not just the launch story.
• **Data flow** — What user data gets sent to Claude? What comes back? Where is it stored? Engineers think in systems, so show them the data architecture.

The biggest mistake PMs make with engineers is overselling. Saying "Claude can handle that" when you haven't tested the edge case destroys credibility. Instead, be precise: "Claude handles standard cases well. We need to test these three edge cases and build fallbacks."

**Talking to Executives**

Executives operate at a different altitude. They don't need to understand how Claude processes tokens — they need to understand why this investment matters and what success looks like.

• **Business outcomes over technology** — "We're reducing support resolution time by 35%" beats "We're implementing an LLM-powered response suggestion system" every time.
• **Competitive context** — Where are competitors with AI? Are we keeping pace, catching up, or leading? Executives think in competitive terms.
• **Risk and timeline** — What could go wrong? How long until we see results? Executives value PMs who present a realistic plan over an optimistic one.
• **Investment framing** — Frame AI costs as investments with expected returns, not as expenses. "This feature costs $50K/year in API fees and saves $400K/year in support costs" is a story executives can act on.

**Talking to Customers**

Customers are the hardest audience because their expectations are shaped by AI hype in the media. They've seen demos that look like magic and assume your product should work the same way.

• **Focus on the outcome, not the technology** — Customers don't care that you're using Claude. They care that their reports get written faster, their data gets analyzed automatically, or their questions get answered instantly.
• **Set honest expectations** — "This feature handles about 80% of cases automatically and gives you a starting point for the rest" is better than promising perfection and delivering frustration.
• **Emphasize user control** — Customers feel more comfortable with AI when they know they're in charge. "You can review, edit, or dismiss any AI suggestion" reduces anxiety and builds trust.

sections.title: Framing AI Capabilities Without Overpromising
sections.content:
The language you use to describe AI features shapes expectations, and mismatched expectations are the primary source of AI feature disappointment. Overpromise and users feel betrayed. Underpromise and stakeholders won't fund the project. The sweet spot is confident honesty.

**The Capability Framing Framework**

For every AI feature, prepare three statements:

• **What it does well** — Lead with the specific strength, backed by evidence. "Claude accurately categorizes 85% of support tickets into the correct department based on our testing with 2,000 historical tickets."
• **Where it has limits** — Be direct about boundaries without being apologetic. "It's less reliable with tickets that span multiple departments or contain highly technical jargon specific to our enterprise clients."
• **How it improves** — Show the path forward. "We're iterating on the prompts weekly based on miscategorized tickets, and accuracy has improved 12 percentage points in the last month."

This framework works with every audience. Adjust the technical depth, but keep the structure.

**Language Patterns to Use**

Precise language builds trust. Vague language invites misinterpretation.

• Say "assists" instead of "automates" unless the process is truly end-to-end with no human involvement.
• Say "generates a draft" instead of "writes" — it signals that human review is part of the workflow.
• Say "in our testing" or "based on current data" instead of making unconditional claims. Qualifying your statements doesn't make them weaker — it makes them credible.
• Say "improves over time" instead of "learns" — "learns" implies autonomous intelligence that can create unrealistic expectations.

**Language Patterns to Avoid**

Certain phrases reliably cause problems:

• **"AI-powered"** as the only description — This is meaningless without context. Every chatbot is "AI-powered." What specifically does your feature do?
• **"It understands"** — Claude processes text and generates responses. Saying it "understands" your users implies a level of cognition that creates unrealistic expectations.
• **"It never makes mistakes"** — It will. Claiming otherwise sets you up for a trust-destroying moment when the first error surfaces.
• **"It's just like talking to a person"** — It isn't, and users who expect human-level nuance will be disappointed by edge cases.

**The Confidence Spectrum**

Match your confidence level to your evidence:

• **High confidence:** "Based on testing with 5,000 real examples, this feature correctly extracts invoice data 92% of the time." Use this when you have robust testing data.
• **Medium confidence:** "In early testing, this feature shows promising results for summarizing meeting transcripts, with most test users accepting the output with minor edits." Use this for features with limited but positive data.
• **Low confidence:** "We're exploring whether Claude can assist with code review suggestions. Initial experiments look promising, but we need more testing before committing to a timeline." Use this for early-stage explorations.

sections.title: Creating Compelling Demos and Proof-of-Concepts
sections.content:
A great demo can secure buy-in for an AI feature faster than any slide deck. A bad demo can kill a project. The difference usually comes down to preparation, not the underlying technology.

**The Demo Paradox**

AI demos face a unique paradox: if the demo goes perfectly, stakeholders assume the feature is done and wonder why you need more time to ship. If the demo shows errors, stakeholders question whether the feature is viable. You need to thread a needle — showing genuine capability while setting honest expectations.

**Structuring an Effective AI Demo**

The best AI demos follow a specific narrative arc:

• **Start with the pain** — Show the manual workflow first. Let stakeholders feel the tedium, complexity, or time cost of the current process. A 30-second clip of an agent manually searching for the right knowledge base article makes the AI solution feel like a relief.
• **Show the happy path** — Demonstrate the AI feature on a case where it works well. Use a real example, not a contrived one. Stakeholders can smell a cherry-picked demo.
• **Show a realistic challenge** — Intentionally demonstrate a case where the AI needs human judgment. This builds credibility: "Here's a complex ticket where Claude provides a starting point, but the agent adds important nuance." This is not weakness — it's realism.
• **End with the improvement story** — Show how the feature gets better over time. If you have before/after data from prompt iterations, present it. "Three weeks ago, acceptance rate was 52%. After prompt refinements based on agent feedback, it's now 71%."

**Building a Proof-of-Concept**

A PoC isn't a demo — it's a time-boxed experiment designed to answer specific questions. Structure your PoC to test the riskiest assumptions first:

• **Define success criteria before building.** What acceptance rate, accuracy, or latency would make this feature worth pursuing? Write it down before the PoC starts.
• **Use real data, not synthetic data.** Claude's performance on carefully crafted test cases doesn't predict its performance on messy real-world inputs. Feed it actual customer support tickets, real meeting transcripts, or genuine user queries.
• **Time-box ruthlessly.** A PoC should take days, not weeks. If you can't demonstrate viability in a few days of prompt engineering and testing, the feature may need a fundamentally different approach.
• **Document failures, not just successes.** The most valuable output of a PoC is understanding what doesn't work. Failure patterns inform your prompt strategy, guardrail design, and scope decisions for the real feature.

**Common Demo Mistakes**

• **Live demos without a backup** — Claude's responses vary. Always have pre-recorded outputs as a backup for high-stakes presentations.
• **Demoing on edge cases** — Start with common, representative examples. Edge cases are important but shouldn't be the first thing stakeholders see.
• **Ignoring latency** — If your demo has a visible delay, address it. "Response time in production will be under 2 seconds" prevents stakeholders from fixating on the pause.
• **Skipping the cost conversation** — Stakeholders who see a compelling demo will ask "why not roll this out to everyone tomorrow?" Have the cost-per-request numbers ready.

sections.title: Handling Pushback and Skepticism
sections.content:
Pushback on AI features is healthy and inevitable. Skeptical stakeholders aren't enemies — they're stress-testing your thinking. The best PMs welcome pushback because it makes the product strategy stronger.

**Common Objections and How to Address Them**

• **"AI is just a fad / hype cycle"**
  Acknowledge the hype honestly: "You're right that there's a lot of hype around AI. That's exactly why we're focused on measurable outcomes, not buzzwords. Our pilot showed a 35% reduction in ticket resolution time — that's not hype, that's a business result." Ground every claim in specific data from your own testing, not industry trends.

• **"What about hallucinations / errors?"**
  Don't minimize this concern — validate it: "Hallucination is a real risk, and we've designed for it. Human review is built into the workflow, Claude never takes autonomous actions on behalf of the user, and we track error rates weekly. Our current hallucination rate on this task is under 3%." Show that you've thought about the failure mode, not just the success case.

• **"This will replace people's jobs"**
  Frame AI as augmentation, not replacement, and be genuine about it: "This feature handles the repetitive parts of the workflow so agents can focus on complex cases that require human judgment. Our pilot agents reported higher job satisfaction because they spent less time on copy-paste tasks." If headcount reduction IS part of the strategy, be honest with leadership about it rather than pretending otherwise.

• **"We don't have the data / infrastructure"**
  Scope the requirements realistically: "Claude works with the data we already have. We don't need a data warehouse or ML pipeline. The first version uses prompt engineering with our existing knowledge base, and we can iterate from there." The Claude API dramatically lowers the infrastructure bar compared to traditional ML.

• **"The cost is too high"**
  Present the full equation: "Each Claude API call costs approximately $0.03 for this use case. An agent handling this task manually costs $0.85 per ticket in salary time. Even at a 60% automation rate, we save $0.48 per ticket. At our volume, that's $180K annually." Always compare AI costs to the current cost of doing business.

**The Skeptic Conversion Playbook**

Some stakeholders won't be convinced by arguments alone. They need to see it work in their context:

• **Offer a time-boxed pilot.** "Let's run this for two weeks with one team and measure the results. If the data doesn't support continuing, we stop." Low commitment reduces resistance.
• **Let skeptics choose the test cases.** When a skeptic picks the hardest examples they can think of, and Claude handles them reasonably well, the credibility boost is enormous.
• **Share raw data, not just summaries.** Skeptics trust data they can interrogate. Give them access to the actual outputs, user feedback, and metrics — not just a polished summary.
• **Find an internal champion.** One respected engineer or team lead who uses the AI feature successfully and speaks positively about it is worth more than any number of slide decks.

sections.title: Write an AI Feature Brief for Different Audiences
sections.content: Practice tailoring your AI communication to different stakeholders by writing the same feature description three different ways.

exercise.prompt:
You're the PM for a Claude-powered feature that automatically summarizes long customer feedback threads (email chains, support conversations, survey responses) into structured insights — key themes, sentiment trends, and recommended actions. The feature has been in pilot for 4 weeks with the Customer Success team.

Pilot results: 73% of summaries accepted without edits, average time saved per feedback review reduced from 12 minutes to 3 minutes, customer success managers report higher confidence in quarterly business reviews because they're reviewing 3x more feedback.

Write three versions of a feature brief — one for each audience:

1. **Engineering Brief** (2-3 paragraphs) — What does the engineering team need to know to scope the production build? Cover integration points, data flow, failure modes, and iteration plan.
2. **Executive Summary** (1 paragraph, 4-5 sentences) — What does your VP of Product need to know to approve funding for the full rollout? Focus on business impact and investment.
3. **Customer Announcement** (1 paragraph, 3-4 sentences) — What do customers need to know? Focus on what the feature does for them and how they stay in control.

For each version, explicitly note: what information you included that's unique to that audience, and what information you deliberately left out and why.
hints: Engineers care about the 27% of summaries that needed edits — what went wrong and how will you address it? They also want to know about model selection, latency requirements, and data handling.
Your VP of Product wants to see the ROI: time saved x number of CSMs x hourly cost. They also want to know the competitive angle — are competitors offering this?
Customers don't care about Claude, acceptance rates, or API costs. They care that their feedback is being heard and acted on, and that they can review what the AI produces.
The deliberate omissions are as important as the inclusions — explain your reasoning for what you left out of each version
exercise.evaluationCriteria: The response should include: (1) An engineering brief that covers integration points (API, data pipeline), failure handling, model selection rationale, latency requirements, and an iteration plan — vague statements like 'we'll use Claude' without specifying the model or architecture are insufficient. (2) An executive summary that quantifies business impact (time saved, coverage increase, projected cost savings at scale), mentions the investment required, and frames the competitive context — should avoid technical jargon. (3) A customer announcement that describes the feature in outcome terms (not technology terms), emphasizes user control and review capability, and sets appropriate expectations — should not mention Claude, acceptance rates, or internal metrics. (4) For each version, explicit notes on what was included uniquely for that audience and what was deliberately omitted with reasoning. Deduct points for briefs that don't differentiate meaningfully between audiences, that use jargon inappropriate for the audience, or that omit the deliberate inclusion/exclusion analysis.

sections.title: Check Your Understanding
sections.content:
Before moving on, make sure you can answer these questions:

• What is the single most important thing engineers want to know about an AI feature that executives typically don't care about?
• Why is "AI-powered" a poor way to describe a feature to customers?
• When a skeptical stakeholder says "AI is just hype," what's the most effective type of evidence to present?
• What's the risk of giving a flawless AI demo with no errors shown?

sections.title: Key Takeaways
sections.content:
Communicating AI value is a core PM skill that determines whether your AI features get funded, built, and adopted:

• **Know your audience's mental model.** Engineers think in systems and failure modes. Executives think in business outcomes and competitive positioning. Customers think in personal value and control. Translate accordingly.

• **Use the Capability-Limit-Improvement framework.** For every AI feature, be prepared to state what it does well, where it has boundaries, and how it gets better. This structure works with every audience.

• **Precision builds trust, vagueness destroys it.** Say "85% accuracy in testing with 2,000 examples" instead of "very accurate." Say "generates a draft for human review" instead of "writes automatically." Qualified claims are more credible than absolute ones.

• **Welcome pushback.** Skeptical stakeholders sharpen your strategy. Offer time-boxed pilots, share raw data, and let skeptics choose the test cases. Evidence converts skeptics; arguments rarely do.

• **Demos are narratives, not showcases.** Start with the pain, show the capability, acknowledge the limits, and end with the improvement trajectory. A demo that shows one realistic challenge is more credible than a demo that looks perfect.
